local_names.romanized_local: Hā Jiǎo / Ha Gaau / Har Gow

region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; widely consumed in Singapore as a staple yum cha dish
food_subgroup: Steamed dumpling — wheat starch and tapioca starch wrapper filled with whole shrimp/prawn; 4 pieces = 1 bamboo steamer basket = ~60g; each piece ~15g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific class for Cantonese dim sum dumplings or har gau. Closest defensible parent is steamed dumpling. No code is assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dumplings — FoodEx2 matrix code for filled pasta/dumplings (steamed); used as closest defensible anchor for har gau as a steamed filled dumpling product.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR covers filled dumplings broadly; no specific Cantonese dim sum subcode exists. This is the most defensible available anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept exists for har gau or Cantonese steamed shrimp dumpling. Null is appropriate.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of har gau is 4 pieces (1 bamboo steamer basket), weighing approximately 60g, providing approximately 85 kcal, 7.2g protein, and 1.6g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Har Gau (Steamed Shrimp Dumpling) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values per 100g sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for har gau / steamed shrimp dumpling. This is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. Values for trace minerals (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central shrimp and wheat starch component data where HPB does not report them, flagged at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat starch-based products and analogous steamed dumpling wrappers — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for har gau exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~48 (low) is estimated from the closest analogous food: wheat starch-based steamed products. Wheat starch (the primary wrapper ingredient) has a substantially lower GI than regular wheat flour due to removal of gluten proteins, which slows starch digestion. This is consistent with published GI values for wheat starch noodles and steamed wheat starch products (~45–55) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 60g serving = (48 × 11.4g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.5 (low). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: One basket of har gau (60g) contributes approximately 220mg sodium, which is acceptable in isolation, but sodium load increases substantially when consumed with soy dipping sauce (~350mg per teaspoon) or alongside multiple other dim sum dishes at yum cha; patients with hypertension should limit soy sauce use and monitor total meal sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~48), low GL per serving (~5.5), high shrimp protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Har gau is one of the most favourable dim sum choices for patients with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (~48), low glycemic load per basket (~5.5), and high-quality shrimp protein (7.2g per 60g serving); the primary clinical concern at yum cha is cumulative carbohydrate from multiple dishes rather than har gau itself.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8g/100g), steamed preparation, moderate cholesterol from shrimp
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Har gau is steamed (not fried), low in saturated fat (0.48g per 60g serving), and provides selenium (10.8mcg per serving) and shrimp-derived omega-3 fatty acids; cholesterol is moderate (~51mg per 60g basket) and consistent with current evidence that dietary cholesterol from seafood has minimal impact on cardiovascular risk in the context of a low saturated fat diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Selenium (18mcg/100g), complete seafood protein, low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Shrimp in har gau provides selenium (18mcg/100g), a trace mineral with antioxidant properties relevant to inflammation management in rheumatoid arthritis, along with complete amino acid protein and low saturated fat; omega-3 content is present but lower than oily fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Har Gau (虾饺 / 蝦餃, also romanized as Har Gow or Ha Gaau) is the benchmark dish of Cantonese dim sum cuisine, widely regarded as the technical test of a dim sum chef's skill. It consists of whole fresh shrimp or prawn encased in a thin, translucent wrapper made from wheat starch and tapioca starch — a key distinction from regular wheat flour dumplings — which gives the skin its characteristic semi-transparent appearance and slightly lower glycemic index. The filling typically includes large plump prawns seasoned with ginger, bamboo shoots, and a touch of lard for texture. The dumplings are steamed in bamboo baskets and served in portions of 4 pieces (~60g per basket). Nutritionally, har gau is one of the most favourable dim sum items: approximately 85 kcal per 4-piece basket, 7.2g protein, 1.6g fat, 11.4g carbohydrate, GI ~48 (low), and GL ~5.5 (low). It is steamed rather than fried, making it lower in fat than many other dim sum options. The primary clinical concern is sodium — 220mg per basket before soy dipping sauce, which can double the sodium load. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, har gau is a recommended dim sum choice due to its low GI and low GL. It is a staple of Singapore yum cha culture, traditionally consumed during weekend brunch at Chinese restaurants. Compare with lo mai gai (sticky rice in lotus leaf, GI ~100, GL ~40 per parcel) — 1 basket of har gau has approximately 7 times lower glycemic load than 1 lo mai gai.
common_user_queries: how many calories in har gau

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. GI estimated from closest analogous wheat starch-based steamed products in the University of Sydney GI Database at confidence 0.9. Trace mineral values (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central shrimp and wheat starch component data at confidence 0.9. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchored to FoodEx2 A0FHR (closest defensible parent).
notes: Trans fat is 0.0g per 100g — confirmed zero per HPB source; har gau is steamed and contains no hydrogenated fats. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by HPB source; set to -1.0. Water content not reported by source; set to -1.0. Halal status is not confirmed as lard is a traditional ingredient in some preparations; is_halal set to false pending restaurant-specific verification. GI value of 48 is an analogous estimate from wheat starch steamed products; no direct har gau GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney database. Portions in input had duplicate entries and conflicting piece counts (3 pieces vs 4 pieces); canonical serving standardized to 4 pieces = 1 basket = 60g per HPB Singapore and the food name. The 75g / 3-piece variant is noted as an alternative portion used by some establishments.

q1_food_identity.notes: canonical English name present and multilingual identity satisfied

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: core macros and nutrient source integrity pass

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: secondary nutrient fields are present and numeric

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: GI, GI category, GL, and GI source are complete

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: alias coverage is adequate and multilingual alias consistency passes

improvement_actions_taken: Restructured input JSON into RESULT template format.
Added mandatory Hindi local name (उबली हुई झींगा डम्पलिंग) which was absent from input.
Corrected health_context to use required keys: hypertension, type2_diabetes, cardiovascular_disease with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.
Added cardiovascular_disease health context entry which was absent from input.
Mapped per_100g field names to RESULT template field names (e.g. energy→energy_kcal, protein→protein_g).
Set trans_fat_g=0.0 with confirmed zero justification in notes.
Set monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g to -1.0 (not reported by source).
Resolved duplicate and conflicting portion entries; standardized canonical serving to 4 pieces = 60g.
Populated FoodEx2 A0FHR as closest defensible ontology anchor.
Expanded natural_language_description to exceed 200 characters with clinical and comparative content.
Cleaned region_tags and country_of_origin fields which contained narrative text instead of structured values.

Iteration 3: Audited flat aliases array to ensure every entry in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) is present in the flat aliases list. Added missing entries: Xiājiǎo, Hā Jiǎo, ஹர் கோ (வேகாத இறால் கொழுக்கட்டை), उबली हुई झींगा डम्पलिंग, har gau dim sum. Verified bidirectional consistency between flat aliases and aliases_by_language to resolve Q8 failure from iteration 2.
